question: Each year, cardiologists surgically repair thousands of clogged coronary arteries only to have many of these arteries narrow again (restenose) soon after surgery. A study sponsored by the NIH was conducted to help determine whether infection with a common type of virus, cytomegalovirus (CMY), contributed to coronary restenosis. Forty-nine of the subjects showed serological evidence of CMV infection, while 26 showed no such evidence. In the CMV+ group, 21 individuals restenosed within 6 months of atherectomy. In the CMV — group, 2 individuals restenosed.
answer:
(a) Risk in the CMV+group $\hat{p}_1= rac{21}{49}=0.4286$

Risk in the CVM - group $\hat{p}_2= rac{2}{26}=0.0769$
Risk difference $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2=0.4286-0.0769=0.3517$
(b) $95 \%$ confidence by the plus-four method:

$$
 egin{aligned}
&  ar{P}_1= rac{22}{51}=0.4314 \
&  ar{P}_2= rac{3}{28}=0.1071 \
& S E=0.0907
\end{aligned}
$$

$95 \%$ confidence interval for $p_1-p_2=(0.4314-0.1071) \pm(1.96)(0.0907)=$

$$
0.3243 \pm 0.1778=(0.1465,0.5021)
$$


Explanation of the confidence interval in lay terms: We have $95 \%$ confidence in the technique used to calculate the confidence interval. The technique says that CMV increases the risk of restenosis by between $14.7 \%$ and $50.2 \%$.

90 \% 	ext { C.I. }=0.181 	ext { to } 0.479

Continuity-corrected (Newcombe's method 11):

$$
 egin{aligned}

& 95 \% 	ext { C.I. }=0.118 	ext { to } 0.513 \

The $95 \%$ confidence interval by the Wilson score method, not continuity-corrected (bold face) most closely corresponds with our plus-four method.
